title: Data Update 8 for 2026: Time for Harvesting - Dividends and Buybacks
gist: Damodaran argues dividend policy is dysfunctional (driven by inertia and peer-group me-tooism rather than free cash flow to equity), buybacks now dominate global cash return and most anti-buyback and pro-buyback 'myths' don't hold up empirically, and dividend-based value-investing strategies (Ben Graham style) are increasingly concentrated in a shrinking, mature-sector universe.

themes.theme: The Dividend Decision and Why It's Dysfunctional

insights.claim: Every firm makes three core corporate finance decisions in sequence: the investment decision (projects earning above the hurdle rate), the financing decision (debt/equity mix), and the dividend decision (how much cash to return to owners) - with dividends properly being the residual left after the first two decisions.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Judge a company's payout against what's actually left over after investment and financing needs, not as an independent, sacrosanct commitment.
insights.locator: I start my corporate finance classes with a description of three core decisions

insights.claim: Dividend policy is dysfunctional because it is driven by two real-world forces rather than residual cash flow logic: inertia (this year's dividend is set equal to last year's) and 'me-tooism' (managers set payout based on peer-group norms, e.g., banks and utilities pay high dividends and tech pays low/none simply because that is sector practice).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Peer-mimicking payout policy creates mispriced outliers - fast-growing banks or mature tech firms trapped in a payout norm that doesn't fit their life-cycle stage.
insights.quote: the word I would use to describe dividend policy, at least when it comes to conventional dividends, is 'sticky'
insights.locator: Inertia / Me-tooism sections

insights.claim: In every single year from 1988 to 2025, the percentage of US companies that kept dividends unchanged from the prior year exceeded the percentage that changed them, and when dividends were changed, increases outnumbered decreases.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Because cuts are so rare, a dividend cut when it does happen carries outsized negative signaling value and should be treated as a major red flag, not routine noise.
insights.locator: track the percentage of companies that increase dividends, decrease dividends or leave them unchanged

insights.claim: When sustaining or growing the dividend becomes an end in itself, firms become 'dividend monsters' that distort their investing and financing decisions - turning away good investment projects or borrowing more than they should purely to keep the dividend flowing.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Screen for firms whose dividend exceeds what free cash flow to equity can sustainably support - a signal of capital misallocation or creeping leverage risk.
insights.quote: you unleash dividend monsters, where investing and financing decisions are skewed to meet dividend needs
insights.locator: When maintaining or increasing dividends become the end game

themes.theme: Why Buybacks Won (and Why the Standard Explanations Are Wrong)

insights.claim: In the last decade, more than 60% of the total cash US companies returned to shareholders took the form of buybacks rather than dividends, a reversal from most of the last century when dividends were the primary or only return mechanism.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Buybacks are now the dominant capital-return channel, so cash-return analysis that focuses only on dividend yield materially understates shareholder distributions.
insights.locator: in the last decade, more than 60% of cash returned to shareholders took the form of buybacks

insights.claim: The primary reason buybacks have overtaken dividends is their flexibility - companies can reverse or pause them when macro conditions worsen (as happened in 2008 and 2020) - not stock-based compensation incentives or investor tax preferences, both of which are weaker explanations than commonly assumed.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't expect executive comp reform or tax policy changes to meaningfully shrink buyback volume; watch macro conditions instead, since that's the actual lever companies pull.
insights.locator: The primary reason, in my view, is that buybacks, unlike dividends, are flexible

insights.claim: Buybacks are just as prevalent at companies with no or very low stock-based compensation as at companies with high stock-based compensation, and buybacks have kept climbing even as firms shift from stock options to restricted stock (which removes the price-support incentive) - undermining the argument that manager comp is driving the buyback boom.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Discounts a popular governance narrative that blames buybacks on executives propping up their own option value.
insights.locator: buybacks have continued to climb, and they are just as high at companies that have no or very low stock based compensation

insights.claim: Changes in tax law over the last two decades have reduced or eliminated the tax disadvantage dividends once had relative to capital gains, weakening the argument that investor tax preferences are what pushed companies from dividends toward buybacks.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Tax-driven explanations for the dividend-to-buyback shift are largely obsolete and shouldn't guide expectations of future policy shifts.
insights.locator: changes in tax law in the last two decades have reduced, if not eliminated, the tax disadvantages

themes.theme: FCFE and the Life-Cycle Framework for Cash Return

insights.claim: The correct measure of how much cash a firm can sustainably return is free cash flow to equity (FCFE) - equity earnings plus non-cash charges, minus capex and working-capital changes, adjusted for net debt cash flows - and paying dividends or buying back stock when FCFE is negative violates the basic rule that if you're in a hole, you should stop digging.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Use FCFE, not net income or cash on hand, as the benchmark for judging whether a company's dividend or buyback is sustainable.
insights.locator: free cashflow to equity

insights.claim: Cash return policy should track a company's life-cycle stage: young companies typically have negative FCFE and need equity infusions, young growth companies are self-funding but can't afford dividends, mature growth companies have cash to return but prefer buybacks for flexibility, and mature stable companies - with low reinvestment needs and predictable earnings - are the 'sweet spot' for dividend paying.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Use life-cycle stage as a screen: a young or high-growth firm paying steady dividends, or a mature firm hoarding cash, is 'acting its age wrong' and worth scrutinizing.
insights.locator: you can already see why you should expect cash return policies to change over a company's life cycle

insights.claim: Companies that 'refuse to act their age' - young firms paying dividends/buybacks, or mature firms hoarding cash - damage themselves and their shareholders; excess cash hoarding can trigger investors to apply a 'lack-of-trust' discount when they don't trust management's capital discipline.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A persistently rising cash balance at a mature company, absent clear reinvestment plans, is a candidate for a market discount and activist pressure.
insights.quote: you may punish a company for holding too much cash, effectively apply a "lack-of-trust" discount to the cash
insights.locator: companies that refuse to act their age

themes.theme: 2025 Global Dividend Data and the Decline of Income Investing

insights.claim: In 2025, the median dividend payout ratio (among dividend payers) was about 35% for US companies and 59% for global companies, with 12% of US firms and 14% of global firms paying out more than 100% of earnings as dividends.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A payout ratio over 100% is a flag to check whether it's a temporary bad-earnings year, deliberate leverage-up, or partial liquidation.
insights.locator: the median payout ratio is about 35% (59%) for US (global) companies

insights.claim: In 2025, the median dividend yield among dividend-paying firms was 1.10% in the US versus 2.43% globally, and the share of firms paying dividends has dropped below 30% in the US, with less than half of firms paying dividends globally.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Dividend-yield screens now apply to a shrinking, non-representative slice of the equity market, especially in the US.
insights.locator: the median dividend yield was 1.10% for the former and 2.43% for the latter

insights.claim: With the US cost of equity at about 8.50% at the start of 2026 and the median dividend yield near 1.10%, equity investors are now almost entirely dependent on price appreciation, not dividends, for their expected returns.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Income-oriented equity strategies can no longer rely on dividend yield to deliver a meaningful share of expected total return.
insights.locator: that expected return was about 8.50% at the start of 2026

insights.claim: Dividends made up roughly half of the S&P 500's expected total return in 1960 but had fallen to less than 15% of total return by 2025, reflecting a multi-decade structural decline in the dividend yield's contribution to equity returns.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The long-run shift from dividend-driven to appreciation-driven returns is structural, not cyclical, and shouldn't be expected to revert.
insights.locator: In 1960, about half of your expected return on stocks came from dividends

insights.claim: In 2025, financials, real estate and utilities had the highest percentage of dividend-paying firms and the highest payout ratios, with energy and real estate paying out more than 200% of net income and yielding over 6%, while technology and communication services had the lowest dividend participation and yields.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Dividend-yield-based sector exposure is now effectively a bet concentrated in financials, real estate, utilities and energy.
insights.locator: the sectors with the highest percentage of firms paying dividends are financials, real estate and utilities

insights.claim: Ben Graham's classic 'principal protection' strategy of buying large dividend-paying firms and holding for the long term has 'hit a wall,' because any dividend-focused strategy today necessarily concentrates a portfolio into mature and increasingly declining sectors like real estate, banking and utilities as the dividend-paying universe shrinks.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Classic dividend-value investing frameworks need updating for a market where dividend payers are a shrinking, sector-concentrated minority.
insights.quote: Ben Graham's strategy of principal protection was built around buying large dividend paying firms and holding on for the long term and it has hit a wall
insights.locator: put old time value investing to the test

insights.claim: In 2025, US companies bought back $1.153 trillion in stock (about 60% of all cash returned); Canada, the UK and Japan returned more than 35% via buybacks; the EU returned about 29%; while Africa & the Middle East, Eastern Europe and much of South/Southeast Asia returned relatively little via buybacks, due to weaker corporate governance and regulatory restrictions.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Buybacks are no longer a US-only phenomenon, so global cash-return comparisons that ignore buybacks understate distributions outside the US too, though governance-weak regions remain an exception.
insights.locator: Companies in the United States are still in the lead in the buyback race

insights.claim: Buybacks do not reduce aggregate corporate investment; they redirect capital away from mature companies with few investment opportunities and, via the market, into younger, higher-growth companies that need the cash, since buyback proceeds mostly flow to investors who reinvest them elsewhere rather than disappearing from the economy.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The 'buybacks starve the economy of investment' critique misidentifies where investment happens - it shifts location (mature to young firms), not aggregate level.
insights.locator: Buybacks redirect corporate investment from mature companies to growth businesses

insights.claim: Younger companies, broken down by corporate age decile, return less cash overall (in both dividends and buybacks, as a percent of market cap) than older companies, consistent with cash rotating from mature to young businesses via the buyback channel.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Supports treating buybacks as a capital-recycling mechanism from old to new businesses rather than a drain on total investment.
insights.locator: younger companies are not only less likely to buy back stock

insights.claim: Aggregate debt-to-capital ratios for US non-financial firms have declined over the last four decades even as buybacks surged, and US companies that bought back stock in 2025 had lower debt-to-EBITDA and debt-to-capital ratios than companies that did not buy back stock - contradicting the claim that buybacks are debt-funded and over-levering companies.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The 'buybacks are funded with debt' critique doesn't hold in the aggregate data - leverage concerns should focus on individual outlier firms, not buybacks as a class.
insights.locator: companies that bought back stock had much lower debt loads than the companies that did not

insights.claim: Warren Buffett was a late adopter of buybacks at Berkshire Hathaway and imposed a self-discipline of only buying back stock when it traded below intrinsic value - a constraint most companies don't impose, despite lacking Buffett's credibility to make that call.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Few companies have the valuation credibility to justify Buffett-style price-disciplined buybacks, so most buyback programs likely lack this safeguard.
insights.locator: Warren Buffett was late to the buyback party

insights.claim: Buybacks, at any price, cannot add or destroy value - they can only transfer wealth between shareholders: an overpriced buyback transfers wealth from remaining holders to those who sell, and an underpriced buyback transfers wealth from sellers to those who remain, so both the 'buybacks destroy value' and 'buybacks create value' narratives misclassify buybacks as investment projects rather than cash returns.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: As a shareholder, whether a buyback helps or hurts you depends entirely on your own decision to sell or hold, not on some inherent value creation/destruction by the company.
insights.quote: a cash return can neither add nor destroy value, but it can transfer wealth
insights.locator: Myth 1.4 / Myth 2.1

insights.claim: A fair-value buyback leaves per-share value unchanged because share count and firm value shrink proportionally; any real effect on value per share comes from a secondary channel - the buyback's impact on the debt-to-capital mix and cost of capital - and the observed empirical link between buybacks and rising stock prices may reflect selection bias (under-levered, cash-rich firms doing most buybacks) rather than a causal effect.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't assume buyback announcements are inherently bullish signals - the correlation with rising stock prices may just reflect which firms choose to buy back, not the buyback's own effect.
insights.locator: Myth 2.2

themes.theme: Dividend Dysfunction by the Numbers, and the Verdict on Cash-Return Investing

insights.claim: Globally in 2025, about 18% of money-losing companies still paid dividends and about 70% of money-making companies did; separately, about 37% of companies that returned any cash (dividends or buybacks) had negative FCFE, while 66% of companies with positive FCFE returned cash.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Roughly a fifth of dividend payers are cash-flow-negative and roughly a third of cash returners overall have negative FCFE - a sizeable minority of the market is returning cash it arguably can't sustainably afford.
insights.locator: Across the globe in 2025, almost 18% of money-losing companies paid dividends

insights.claim: Investment strategies built entirely around cash return - whether high-dividend-yield screens or concentrated dividend portfolios like the Dogs of the Dow - are likely to 'go off the tracks' because they misread a stock as a bond substitute (dividends replacing coupons plus some appreciation) rather than an equity claim primarily dependent on price appreciation.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Reassess dividend-yield-driven allocation strategies given how small a share of total equity return dividends now represent.
insights.quote: A stock is not a bond, where dividends replace coupons, and you get some price appreciation on top, and treating it as such will only create disappointment
insights.locator: Conclusion
